item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seems quite similar to SCP-2821 but still quite interesting in itself especially with all the talk about the different kinds of devices the foundation has created. It makes you realize how for every step we make in progress to contain SCPS we create one or two. Also I think Dr. Kraas is being influenced by a possible congnitohazard but ok.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone actually liked it! ;_; Happy day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was actually so well written that I figured it out right away just from reading it, even before I read the spoilers in the author post. There's a ton of SCPs complicated like this one that just go way over my head and i have to read half the discussion page to figure them out.

Good show, easy +1.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: Not really meant to be magical obsession (see SCP-3454 for that!), but it's possible it could be. More, the 'what's going on' is related to the title of the article, but no one really seems to accept that, so oh well I guess.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: Agreed. No vote for me because I like a lot of what is done here, but this reminds me of http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-3107. in 3107, the Foundation realizes that the researchers are obsessed and need to stop. anomalous obsession is the only reason I can see they wouldn't stop the damn hume testing…maybe that's intentional, but its written more like "they are just idiots"

item.title: No one's gonna care, but...
item.content:encoded: Had a weird dream last night, so I decided to add to this. Maybe I should have made it its own skip. Oh well.

I downvoted this a while ago, and I realized that I never explained why. This isn't an terrible skip by any means, and I do think it needs an explanation about why I didn't like it:

My dislike boils down to me thinking that the Foundation is really just holding the idiot ball way too hard in this. I could tell, just after a quick read of the scp that the Hume measurings were causing the reality fluctuations. It boggles the mind that the Foundation - the scientists who are actually dealing with this, and doubtlessly have considerably more information than is here - can't figure it out.

The idea that the Hume readings were causing the fluxes is way to visible for me as a reader, but is only barely addressed in the article with a simple "Research is ongoing" comment in the S.C.P.s.

On the whole, I think the article is executed well, and was an interesting read.

It's just that the Foundation is really stupid here and I can't accept that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think there is some room for improvement, but a +1 nonetheless.

It seems to me that this piece is setting up all the quantified measures on anomalies (Hume readings, SRAs, etc.) as being fundamentally misinterpreted in some way. This may just be because of my viewpoint on the subject - I don't really like the idea that anomalies break the laws of physics simply by being "less real", because that somehow implies them as being "more fake". It also means they're all a result of the same cause, which is simply being too different from limited human understanding of the underpinning functions of the Universe. It suggests that what we know now is complete and perfect, and that simply isn't scientific.

This challenges the assertion, though, and suggests that anomalies can't just be fixed by shooting "realness" at them. I'd like to see more like this, like maybe finding a new skip that completely defies explanation but reads as normal compared to background reality levels. Keep up the good work.

item.title: My thoughts
item.content:encoded: Its a good read and story. The end encapsulates it, the two different views of anomalies and such. But that middle part with the random stuff which can be seen is just too random. It put me in mind of escalations, but it never went anywhere. Also, the idiot ball moment where, apparently, every scientists is in the same room with the damn thing seems wrong. A Skip stays behind bulletproof glass, not a few feet away from you, where they can suck you into the void of space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was my one fear with this article. All I can say is they're too focused on "contain the anomaly" to really see what's going on, and I tried to lampshade that as much as possible in the end notes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what you were going for, but I have a very hard time accepting articles that rely on the Foundation hiding the idiotball. Every time there was a "no activations" bit, I lost more of my disbelief that they wouldn't stop and try to figure out why.

Also: "cannot be anticipated" is a bad line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hah! I put that in there while adding the thing I forgot. (See above.) No wonder this is doing so badly, I obviously can't write skips! XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For what it's worth, I like this as an article in it's own right, and it's worth a +1. The bit in the spoiler in the author post (which I did catch onto) is what really sold me. They just can't help themselves, can they? The subtext critiquing SRAs and friends doesn't really sway me one way or the other, since they, as a concept, don't really bother me. They can be used to good effect or to bad, just like anything else. If used as just another tool that the Foundation has at its disposal (and preferably used when other solutions have failed), they are fine. When they are a be-all-and-end-all deus ex machina that magically makes everything better because hand-waving magitech reasons…not so much.

(That being said, there is a stray SCP-XXXX in the last collapsible that I am relatively certain shouldn't be there.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops, I will change that. :O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote from me for now. It just doesn't feel that cohesive of an SCP - the concept of 'anomaly that does random things when measured' is a bit too nebulous for my liking. If I could give this half an up vote, I would. Hell, maybe it's just a preference thing for me.

Also, not sure who that Lazar is, but it's not me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For all its worth, it definitely ended on a good note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An interesting concept and a well executed, lengthy article.

Well done +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't wrap my head around this sentiment. It's like saying "I conceived it as a reaction to the preponderance of magitech things like pyrex that make it far too easy for the Foundation to contain acids."

SRAs are a literary device, and exist for the sake of letting us write stories a certain way, and as writers we can decide whether/how we want them to exist. It also makes sense to criticize their usage, even though said usage is sensible from an in-universe perspective, because they are fake and not real.

Pyrex is a real-life material, there are very solid reasons for its immunity to acids, and whether it makes containing them more interesting is totally irrelevant. It can only really be criticized on the grounds of efficacy, because it is real and not fake.

Without SRAs, we'd be missing some really interesting and quality stories that are only possible because of them.

Maybe, but that doesn't mean that they're above reproach. The implicit statements they make about the Foundationverse, and the way they shift how stories are written, aren't straightforwardly positive — attributing a diverse array of anomalies to 'reality bending', which can be compensated for via technology, does (as TL333s said and the article implies) undercut the sense of wonder that a sci-fi/fantasy kitchen sink setting like this can evoke.

And really, I think the stories that absolutely need SRAs to work (i.e. couldn't have their anomalous effects tweaked to make more conventional containment methods effective) are fewer than one might think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to call differing headcanons, because I complained about this precise thing on your most recent article.

+ This went on rather a bit
- Sorry

I've always seen the Foundation as trying to remain separate from the anomalous, likely as part of the whole "maintain normalcy" idea. Serpent's Hand may use anomalies to their benefit, the GOC might as well, before shooting them in the back of the head, but for the Foundation, that kind of thing really runs counter to everything they're trying to accomplish and happens only in the direst of circumstances.

The Foundation is Old Man Science, poking his head into the anomalous community and waving his cane, telling the kids to knock off the magic bullshit. They don't employ magicians, they don't use talismans: that's left to the GoIs, that's what separates them from the Foundation. The old adage about advanced tech looking like magic applies, but affecting reality itself seems to me to be the purview of, y'know, reality benders.

Really, I'm more upset about the idea that "the anomalous" runs on a definite, quantifiable set of rules, which it would have to for something like an SRA to function. That a giant arm trapped in a drainage pipe, an endless stairwell home to a malevolent entity and half a cat are all basically the same thing when you get right down to it. That's what this is really about: the Foundation has spent too much time thinking they understand everything, when really, they don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the first one, likely because you don't just go mucking around inside anomalies right off the bat. For the third, eh, I dunno, gravity? I just realized I actually forgot to add something into this, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I conceived it as a reaction to the preponderance of magitech things like the Scranton Reality Anchor that make it far too easy for the Foundation to contain anomalies.

I can't wrap my head around this sentiment. It's like saying "I conceived it as a reaction to the preponderance of magitech things like pyrex that make it far too easy for the Foundation to contain acids."

(Think about how many would be completely uncontained if not for SRAs and the like.)

And how is this a bad thing? Generally speaking, people don't usually write completely uncontainable skips because that defeats the point of a Special Containment Procedures article. Without SRAs, we'd be missing some really interesting and quality stories that are only possible because of them.

This article is essentially a spiteful attack on the scientific method itself. It's the skip equivalent of going "checkmate, atheists!".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was about to comment "was I supposed to realize that it's activ…" and then read spoilers.

Good job +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for some juicy containment procedures, everything that followed was sufficient to keep it there.

Nitpick:

For the first and the third entry… why didn't they try to deactivate it then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to A Random Day, Roget, Kola, AbsentmindedNihilist, Lazar Lyusternik and at least one other person from chat who I forgot to note. Thanks for putting up with my constant begging for draft reviews!

Anyone searching for answers to inconsistencies, errors or anything else out of the ordinary should try rereading the article, and then clicking the collapsible.

+ Spoilers and commentary
- Hide block

First off, the SERS was created by TyGently, and I thank him for letting me poop on it. The exploration log was inspired by (i.e., completely ripped off from) this post. When I saw it, I knew it was SCP fuel, and it fit here perfectly.

As for what's going on, well, there's like 85% chaff in this article, for a good reason: the Foundation has no idea what's going on while being completely assured that they have the situation under control. Put simply, every time they take a Hume reading of the anomaly, it grows and goes active. If they stopped trying to actively contain it, it would do nothing could be considered Safe. I hope this comes through, I tried to leave a lot of bread crumbs for the reader. D:

This article has been published about three years too late. >.> I conceived it as a reaction to the preponderance of magitech things like the Scranton Reality Anchor that make it far too easy for the Foundation to contain anomalies. (Think about how many would be completely uncontained if not for SRAs and the like.) The idea that "anomaly" is a problem to which a single solution can be applied, frankly, ridiculous, and I wanted to encapsulate that ridiculousness in an article.

So yeah, most of this is just stuff you'd expect to see in a somewhat lengthy SCP article — exploration logs, encounters with weird stuff — with a few nuggets of what's actually going on hidden within. This is one of my longer articles, and also, my first Keter. :V Thank you for reading.
